title: Queue - Stack conversion
description: Convert queue to stack and stack to queue using basic operations

examples.explanation: Step 1: Queue=[1,2,3], Stack=[]. Rotate queue: dequeue 1→enqueue back→Queue=[2,3,1], dequeue 2→enqueue back→Queue=[3,1,2]. Dequeue last (3)→push to stack→Stack=[3], Queue=[1,2]. Step 2: Queue=[1,2], Stack=[3]. Rotate: dequeue 1→enqueue back→Queue=[2,1]. Dequeue last (2)→push→Stack=[3,2], Queue=[1]. Step 3: Queue=[1], Stack=[3,2]. No rotation needed (size=1). Dequeue 1→push→Stack=[3,2,1], Queue=[].

Step 1: Queue=[10,20], Stack=[]. Rotate: dequeue 10→enqueue back→Queue=[20,10]. Dequeue last (20)→push→Stack=[20], Queue=[10]. Step 2: Queue=[10], Stack=[20]. No rotation. Dequeue 10→push→Stack=[20,10], Queue=[].

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n²) for Queue to Stack
approaches.summary: To convert a queue to a stack, repeatedly dequeue all elements except the last one, enqueue them back, and then push the last dequeued element onto the stack. This process is repeated for each element, resulting in quadratic time complexity due to multiple passes through the queue.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Convert Queue to Stack Using Brute Force O(n²)
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of converting a queue to a stack using repeated element rotation.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires transforming a queue's FIFO structure into a stack's LIFO structure using only basic queue operations (enqueue, dequeue, size/isEmpty) and stack operations (push, pop, peek, isEmpty). The brute force approach works by repeatedly rotating the queue to access elements in reverse order. The intuition is that to get the last element of the queue (which should be the first in the stack), we need to dequeue all elements except the last one, then push that last element onto the stack. By repeating this process for each element, we effectively reverse the order. The algorithm: 1) While the queue is not empty: a) Dequeue all elements except the last one, enqueuing them back immediately. b) Dequeue the last element and push it onto the stack. 2) Return the stack. Time complexity is O(n²) because for each of n elements, we perform up to n-1 operations to rotate the queue. Space complexity is O(n) for the output stack. This connects to the key subroutine of rotating the queue to access the last element. Common mistakes include not handling empty queues, incorrect loop conditions for rotation, and modifying the original queue when it should be preserved. Real code pattern: def queue_to_stack(queue): stack = [] while not queue.empty(): for _ in range(queue.size() - 1): queue.enqueue(queue.dequeue()) stack.append(queue.dequeue()) return stack

examples.trace: Step 1: Queue=[1,2,3], Stack=[]. Rotate queue: dequeue 1→enqueue back→Queue=[2,3,1], dequeue 2→enqueue back→Queue=[3,1,2]. Dequeue last (3)→push to stack→Stack=[3], Queue=[1,2]. Step 2: Queue=[1,2], Stack=[3]. Rotate: dequeue 1→enqueue back→Queue=[2,1]. Dequeue last (2)→push→Stack=[3,2], Queue=[1]. Step 3: Queue=[1], Stack=[3,2]. No rotation needed (size=1). Dequeue 1→push→Stack=[3,2,1], Queue=[].

Step 1: Queue=[10,20], Stack=[]. Rotate: dequeue 10→enqueue back→Queue=[20,10]. Dequeue last (20)→push→Stack=[20], Queue=[10]. Step 2: Queue=[10], Stack=[20]. No rotation. Dequeue 10→push→Stack=[20,10], Queue=[].

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Rotate Queue to Access Last Element
ladders.desc: Implement a function to rotate a queue so the last element becomes the front.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core operation in the brute force approach. The intuition is that queues only allow access to the front element, but to build a stack we need the last element first. By rotating the queue (moving elements from front to back), we can bring the last element to the front where it can be dequeued. The algorithm: 1) Get the current size of the queue. 2) For i from 1 to size-1: a) Dequeue the front element. b) Enqueue it back to the rear. After this loop, the original last element is now at the front. Time complexity is O(n) for a queue of size n, as we perform n-1 operations. Space complexity is O(1) extra space. This feeds into the full problem by being the repeated step for each element. Common mistakes include incorrect loop range (should be size-1, not size), not handling empty/single-element queues, and assuming the queue size remains constant during rotation. Real code pattern: def rotate_to_last(queue): size = queue.size() for _ in range(size - 1): queue.enqueue(queue.dequeue())

examples.input: Queue: [1, 2, 3, 4], rotate to bring 4 to front
examples.trace: Size=4. Loop i=1: dequeue 1→enqueue back→Queue=[2,3,4,1]. i=2: dequeue 2→enqueue back→Queue=[3,4,1,2]. i=3: dequeue 3→enqueue back→Queue=[4,1,2,3]. Stop after 3 iterations (size-1=3).

examples.input: Queue: [10, 20], rotate to bring 20 to front
examples.trace: Size=2. Loop i=1: dequeue 10→enqueue back→Queue=[20,10]. Stop after 1 iteration (size-1=1).

ladders.title: Core Logic — Reversing Order via Repeated Element Extraction
ladders.desc: Understand how repeatedly taking the last element reverses a sequence.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the conceptual insight behind the approach. The intuition is that to reverse a sequence (like converting FIFO to LIFO), you can repeatedly remove the last element and collect them in order. For a queue, the 'last element' is not directly accessible, but by rotating the queue, we simulate accessing it. Think of it as peeling layers from the back: each time we take what becomes the last element after rotation, which corresponds to the original elements in reverse order. The algorithm concept: 1) While elements remain: a) Rotate to bring last element to front. b) Remove and store that element. 2) The stored elements are in reverse order. Time complexity arises from n rotations of decreasing size: O(n²). Space is O(n) for storage. This is the central technique that enables the brute force solution. Common mistakes include not seeing why this reverses order, or confusing it with simpler reversal methods. Real code pattern: Concept only—no specific code, but the idea is: reversed_list = [] while queue not empty: rotate queue to last element reversed_list.append(queue.dequeue())

examples.input: Sequence: [1, 2, 3], reverse by taking last repeatedly
examples.trace: Step 1: Take last of [1,2,3]→3, remaining [1,2]. Step 2: Take last of [1,2]→2, remaining [1]. Step 3: Take last of [1]→1, remaining []. Collected: [3,2,1].
Step 1: Take last of [10,20]→20, remaining [10]. Step 2: Take last of [10]→10, remaining []. Collected: [20,10].

ladders.title: Building Block — Iterating Through a Queue with Size Tracking
ladders.desc: Implement a loop that processes each element in a queue while modifying it.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves traversing a queue when its contents are changing during iteration. The intuition is that to process all elements, we need to track how many are left, since standard queue iteration isn't straightforward when elements are being moved. We use the queue's size to control loops. The algorithm: 1) While the queue is not empty: a) Perform operations on the current front element. b) Possibly move it to the back or remove it. 2) Use queue.size() to determine how many times to rotate or process. Time complexity depends on operations inside the loop. Space is O(1) extra. This builds toward the core logic by enabling repeated extraction. Common mistakes include using for loops with fixed ranges that don't account for changing size, or infinite loops from incorrect termination conditions. Real code pattern: while not queue.empty(): current_size = queue.size() # Use current_size for inner operations

examples.input: Queue: [1, 2, 3], process each element by moving to back
examples.trace: While queue not empty: Size=3, dequeue front (1)→enqueue back→Queue=[2,3,1]. Size=3, dequeue front (2)→enqueue back→Queue=[3,1,2]. Size=3, dequeue front (3)→enqueue back→Queue=[1,2,3]. Loop continues infinitely unless we track iterations.

examples.input: Queue: [10, 20], dequeue each without enqueuing back
examples.trace: While queue not empty: Size=2, dequeue 10→Queue=[20]. Size=1, dequeue 20→Queue=[].

testCases.input: Queue: [1, 2], move each to back once
Queue: [3, 1, 4], dequeue and enqueue back twice per element
Queue: [100, 200, 300, 400, 500], move front to back 3 times
Queue: [a, b, c], process each to build a list

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Dequeue and Enqueue Back
ladders.desc: Perform the fundamental queue operation of moving an element from front to back.
ladders.explanation: This is a fundamental operation used in the rotation process. The intuition is that to rotate a queue, we take the front element and put it at the back, effectively shifting all elements one position forward. The algorithm: 1) Dequeue the front element, storing its value. 2) Enqueue that value back into the same queue. This operation preserves all elements but changes their order. Time complexity is O(1) per operation. Space complexity is O(1). This feeds into the building block by being the action performed in loops. Common mistakes include not storing the dequeued value before enqueuing, or enqueuing to a different queue. Real code pattern: element = queue.dequeue() queue.enqueue(element)

examples.input: Queue: [1, 2, 3], dequeue and enqueue back front element
examples.trace: Dequeue front (1)→Queue=[2,3]. Enqueue 1 back→Queue=[2,3,1].
Dequeue front (10)→Queue=[]. Enqueue 10 back→Queue=[10].

testCases.expected: Error or no change (depends on implementation)

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Queue FIFO vs Stack LIFO
ladders.desc: Understand the basic difference between queue (First-In-First-Out) and stack (Last-In-First-Out) data structures.
ladders.explanation: This is the foundational concept for the entire problem. A queue follows FIFO order: the first element added is the first one removed, like a line of people. A stack follows LIFO order: the last element added is the first one removed, like a stack of plates. The intuition is that these are opposite ordering principles. To convert between them, we need to reverse the order of elements. For example, if a queue has elements [1,2,3] in FIFO order (1 is front), the corresponding stack in LIFO order should have 3 at the top, then 2, then 1 at the bottom. This foundational understanding motivates why we need algorithms like brute force rotation. Time and space are not applicable here as it's conceptual. This connects to basic operations by defining what enqueue/dequeue and push/pop do. Common mistakes include confusing which end is accessed in each structure, or misunderstanding the order of operations. Real code pattern: Conceptual—queue: enqueue adds to back, dequeue removes from front; stack: push adds to top, pop removes from top.

examples.input: Queue with enqueue 1,2,3 then dequeue all
examples.trace: Enqueue 1→Queue=[1]. Enqueue 2→Queue=[1,2]. Enqueue 3→Queue=[1,2,3]. Dequeue→1, Queue=[2,3]. Dequeue→2, Queue=[3]. Dequeue→3, Queue=[].

examples.input: Stack with push 1,2,3 then pop all
examples.trace: Push 1→Stack=[1]. Push 2→Stack=[1,2]. Push 3→Stack=[1,2,3]. Pop→3, Stack=[1,2]. Pop→2, Stack=[1]. Pop→1, Stack=[].

approaches.name: Two Stack O(n) for Queue to Stack
approaches.summary: Use two stacks to convert a queue to a stack by first pushing all queue elements onto stack1, then popping from stack1 and pushing onto stack2 to reverse the order, effectively simulating stack behavior. This approach leverages stack operations directly without brute-force passes.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Convert Queue to Stack Using Two Stacks
ladders.desc: Complete implementation to convert a queue to a stack using two stacks with O(n) time and space complexity.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires transforming a queue's FIFO behavior into a stack's LIFO behavior using only stack operations. The intuition is that we can reverse the order of elements twice to achieve the desired transformation. First, we transfer all elements from the queue to stack1, which preserves the queue order but in reverse (since popping from a queue gives front elements, pushing to stack puts them in reverse). Then, we transfer from stack1 to stack2, which reverses the order again, resulting in the original queue order but now accessible in LIFO manner from stack2. This double reversal effectively converts FIFO to LIFO. The algorithm: 1) Initialize two empty stacks. 2) While queue is not empty, dequeue element and push to stack1. 3) While stack1 is not empty, pop from stack1 and push to stack2. 4) stack2 now behaves as the converted stack. Time complexity is O(n) for two passes through n elements. Space complexity is O(n) for storing elements in stacks. Connection to L1: This uses the key subroutine of transferring between stacks. Common mistakes include not handling empty queues correctly or trying to access queue elements directly without dequeuing. Real code pattern: def queue_to_stack(queue): stack1 = []; stack2 = []; while queue: stack1.append(queue.pop(0)); while stack1: stack2.append(stack1.pop()); return stack2.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize stack1=[], stack2=[]. Step 2: Dequeue 1 from queue, push to stack1 → stack1=[1], queue=[2,3]. Step 3: Dequeue 2, push to stack1 → stack1=[1,2], queue=[3]. Step 4: Dequeue 3, push to stack1 → stack1=[1,2,3], queue=[]. Step 5: Pop 3 from stack1, push to stack2 → stack1=[1,2], stack2=[3]. Step 6: Pop 2 from stack1, push to stack2 → stack1=[1], stack2=[3,2]. Step 7: Pop 1 from stack1, push to stack2 → stack1=[], stack2=[3,2,1]. Final stack2 has top=1, popping gives 1,2,3 in LIFO order matching original queue order 1,2,3.

Step 1: stack1=[], stack2=[]. Step 2: Dequeue 10 → stack1=[10], queue=[20]. Step 3: Dequeue 20 → stack1=[10,20], queue=[]. Step 4: Pop 20 from stack1 → stack2=[20], stack1=[10]. Step 5: Pop 10 from stack1 → stack2=[20,10], stack1=[]. Final stack2 has top=10, popping gives 10,20 matching queue order 10,20.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Transfer Elements Between Two Stacks
ladders.desc: Implement a function to move all elements from one stack to another, reversing their order.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine is the core of the conversion process. The intuition is that transferring elements from stack A to stack B reverses their order because stacks are LIFO: popping from A gives the top element, and pushing to B places it at the bottom relative to subsequent elements, effectively inverting the sequence. This reversal is crucial for converting queue order to stack order. The algorithm: 1) While stack A is not empty, pop an element from stack A. 2) Push that element onto stack B. 3) Repeat until stack A is empty. Time complexity is O(n) for n elements, as each element is popped and pushed once. Space complexity is O(1) extra space, but overall O(n) for the stacks. Connection to L0: This subroutine is used twice in the full solution—first to transfer from queue to stack1 (simulated via dequeuing), then from stack1 to stack2. Common mistakes include modifying the original stack while iterating incorrectly or not checking for empty stacks. Real code pattern: def transfer_stack(src, dest): while src: dest.append(src.pop()).

examples.trace: Step 1: Pop 3 from stack1 → stack1=[1,2], push to stack2 → stack2=[3]. Step 2: Pop 2 from stack1 → stack1=[1], push to stack2 → stack2=[3,2]. Step 3: Pop 1 from stack1 → stack1=[], push to stack2 → stack2=[3,2,1]. Final stack2 has elements in reverse order of original stack1.

Step 1: Pop 20 from stack1 → stack1=[10], push to stack2 → stack2=[5,20]. Step 2: Pop 10 from stack1 → stack1=[], push to stack2 → stack2=[5,20,10]. Elements from stack1 are added in reverse order to existing stack2.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Reversing Order Using Double Stack Transfer
ladders.desc: Understand how transferring elements between two stacks reverses their sequence, key to converting FIFO to LIFO.
ladders.explanation: The central insight is that moving elements from one stack to another inverts their order due to the LIFO property. When you pop from stack A (which removes the top element) and push to stack B (which adds to the top), the first popped element ends up at the bottom of stack B, and subsequent elements stack on top, creating a reverse sequence. This double reversal is powerful: if you start with a queue (FIFO order), transferring to stack1 reverses it once, then transferring to stack2 reverses it again, restoring the original order but now in LIFO format. The intuition is akin to flipping a stack of papers twice to get back to the start but upside down. Time and space are O(n) as elements are moved. Connection to L1: This logic is implemented in the transfer subroutine. Common mistakes include not realizing that two transfers are needed or misunderstanding the direction of reversal. Real code pattern: This is conceptual; in practice, use two while loops for the transfers.

examples.output: After double transfer: stack with top=1, popping gives 1,2,3
examples.trace: Step 1: Transfer queue to stack1 (simulated): queue gives 1,2,3 in order; pushing to stack1 results in stack1=[1,2,3] (top=3). This is first reversal: queue order 1,2,3 becomes stack order 3,2,1 in stack1. Step 2: Transfer stack1 to stack2: pop from stack1 gives 3,2,1 in that order; pushing to stack2 results in stack2=[3,2,1] (top=1). This second reversal: stack1 order 3,2,1 becomes stack2 order 1,2,3, which is LIFO relative to original queue.

examples.output: After double transfer: stack with top=10
examples.trace: Step 1: Queue to stack1: push 10 then 20 → stack1=[10,20] (top=20). Step 2: Stack1 to stack2: pop 20 then 10 → stack2=[20,10] (top=10). Final stack2 has elements in order 10,20 accessible as LIFO.

ladders.title: Building Block — Simulating Queue Dequeue with Stack Push
ladders.desc: Learn how to transfer elements from a queue to a stack by repeatedly dequeuing and pushing.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves mimicking the process of moving elements from a queue to a stack. Since we cannot directly access queue elements in bulk, we use a loop to dequeue each element (removing from the front) and push it onto a stack (adding to the top). The intuition is that this operation reverses the order: the first dequeued element becomes the bottom of the stack, and later elements stack on top, inverting the FIFO sequence to a LIFO one. This is the first reversal in the full conversion. The algorithm: while queue is not empty, dequeue an element and push it onto stack. Time complexity is O(n) for n elements, as each dequeue and push is O(1). Space complexity is O(n) for the new stack. Connection to L2: This is the first transfer in the double reversal logic. Common mistakes include not handling empty queues or using incorrect queue operations. Real code pattern: stack = []; while queue: stack.append(queue.pop(0)).

examples.trace: Step 1: Dequeue 1 → queue=[2,3], push 1 → stack=[1]. Step 2: Dequeue 2 → queue=[3], push 2 → stack=[1,2]. Step 3: Dequeue 3 → queue=[], push 3 → stack=[1,2,3]. Final stack has elements in order 1,2,3 with top=3.
Step 1: Dequeue 10 → queue=[20], push 10 → stack=[10]. Step 2: Dequeue 20 → queue=[], push 20 → stack=[10,20]. Stack top is 20.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Push and Pop on a Stack
ladders.desc: Master the fundamental stack operations of adding (push) and removing (pop) elements.
ladders.explanation: This foundational operation involves using a stack data structure, which follows Last-In-First-Out (LIFO) order. Pushing adds an element to the top of the stack, and popping removes the top element. The intuition is that stacks are like a stack of plates: you add to the top and remove from the top. In the conversion problem, we use push to add elements from the queue or another stack, and pop to remove elements for transfer. The algorithm for push: add element to the end of a list (if using list as stack). For pop: remove and return the last element. Time complexity is O(1) for both operations. Space complexity is O(n) for storing elements. Connection to L3: These operations are used in transferring elements between stacks. Common mistakes include popping from an empty stack or using incorrect indices. Real code pattern: stack = []; stack.append(item) for push; stack.pop() for pop.

examples.trace: Step 1: Start with empty stack []. Step 2: Push 5 → stack=[5]. Step 3: Push 10 → stack=[5,10]. Step 4: Pop removes top element 10 → stack=[5], return 10.
Step 1: Initial stack=[1,2]. Step 2: Pop removes top 2 → stack=[1], return 2. Step 3: Push 3 → stack=[1,3]. Step 4: Pop removes top 3 → stack=[1], return 3.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Stack LIFO vs Queue FIFO
ladders.desc: Learn the basic difference between stack (Last-In-First-Out) and queue (First-In-First-Out) data structures.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite concept: understanding how stacks and queues work. A stack is a LIFO structure where the last element added is the first one removed, like a stack of books. A queue is a FIFO structure where the first element added is the first one removed, like a line of people. The intuition is that these orders are opposites: in a stack, you access the most recent item; in a queue, you access the oldest item. This difference is why converting between them requires reversing the order. In the problem, we start with a queue (FIFO) and want a stack (LIFO), so we need to invert the sequence. Time and space are not analyzed here as it's conceptual. Connection to L4: This foundation explains why push/pop operations are used for stacks. Common mistakes include confusing LIFO and FIFO or misapplying operations. Real code pattern: For stack, use list with append and pop; for queue, use list with pop(0) or collections.deque.

examples.input: Stack: push 1, push 2, push 3, then pop three times
examples.trace: Step 1: Push 1 → stack=[1]. Step 2: Push 2 → stack=[1,2]. Step 3: Push 3 → stack=[1,2,3]. Step 4: Pop removes top 3 → stack=[1,2], output 3. Step 5: Pop removes top 2 → stack=[1], output 2. Step 6: Pop removes top 1 → stack=[], output 1. This shows LIFO: last in (3) is first out.

examples.input: Queue: enqueue 1, enqueue 2, enqueue 3, then dequeue three times
examples.trace: Step 1: Enqueue 1 → queue=[1]. Step 2: Enqueue 2 → queue=[1,2]. Step 3: Enqueue 3 → queue=[1,2,3]. Step 4: Dequeue removes front 1 → queue=[2,3], output 1. Step 5: Dequeue removes front 2 → queue=[3], output 2. Step 6: Dequeue removes front 3 → queue=[], output 3. This shows FIFO: first in (1) is first out.

testCases.input: Stack: push a, push b, pop
Stack: push 7, push 8, push 9, pop, pop
Queue: enqueue a, enqueue b, enqueue c, dequeue, dequeue
Stack: push m, push n, pop

approaches.name: Recursive O(n) for Queue to Stack
approaches.summary: Convert a queue to a stack using recursion by dequeuing an element, recursively processing the remaining queue, and then pushing the element onto the stack after the recursive call. This reverses the queue order through the call stack, achieving linear time complexity.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Recursive Queue to Stack Conversion
ladders.desc: Complete implementation converting a queue to a stack using recursion
ladders.explanation: This problem requires converting a queue's FIFO order into a stack's LIFO order using only basic queue operations (enqueue, dequeue, front) and stack operations (push, pop, top). The recursive approach leverages the call stack to reverse the queue's order. Intuition: When we dequeue an element and recursively process the remaining queue, the recursion unwinds in reverse order, allowing us to push elements onto the stack in the reversed sequence. This works because recursion naturally creates a last-in-first-out structure in the call stack, mirroring stack behavior. Step-by-step: 1. Base case: If queue is empty, return. 2. Dequeue front element and store it. 3. Recursively call function on the now-shorter queue. 4. After recursive call returns, push stored element onto stack. This ensures the first dequeued element (original front) gets pushed last, achieving reversal. Time complexity is O(n) as each element is processed once. Space complexity is O(n) due to recursion stack depth equal to queue size. Connection to parent: This is the complete solution using the specified approach. Common mistakes include not handling empty queue properly, forgetting to store dequeued element before recursion, or incorrect base case. Real code pattern: def queue_to_stack(queue): if queue.empty(): return; element = queue.dequeue(); queue_to_stack(queue); stack.push(element);

examples.trace: Step 1: Queue=[1,2,3], element=1 dequeued, recursive call. Step 2: Queue=[2,3], element=2 dequeued, recursive call. Step 3: Queue=[3], element=3 dequeued, recursive call. Step 4: Queue empty, base case returns. Step 5: Push 3 onto stack → Stack=[3]. Step 6: Push 2 onto stack → Stack=[3,2]. Step 7: Push 1 onto stack → Stack=[3,2,1]. Final stack top=1.

Step 1: Queue=[10,20], element=10 dequeued, recursive call. Step 2: Queue=[20], element=20 dequeued, recursive call. Step 3: Queue empty, base case returns. Step 4: Push 20 onto stack → Stack=[20]. Step 5: Push 10 onto stack → Stack=[20,10]. Final stack top=10.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Recursive Reversal Function
ladders.desc: The recursive function that processes queue elements in reverse order
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the approach: a recursive function that takes a queue as input and reverses its order through the call stack. Intuition: By deferring the push operation until after the recursive call, we ensure elements are processed in reverse order of their original queue position. The recursion naturally builds a chain of suspended operations that complete in LIFO order. Step-by-step: 1. Check if queue is empty (base case). 2. Remove front element and store it temporarily. 3. Recursively call the function on the reduced queue. 4. After recursion returns, push stored element onto stack. This creates a reversal because the first element removed gets pushed last. Time complexity: O(n) where n is queue size. Space complexity: O(n) for recursion stack. Connection to L0: This function is called by the main solution to perform the conversion. Common mistakes include modifying the queue before storing element, incorrect base case handling, or not understanding recursion unwinding. Real code pattern: def reverse_queue(queue): if queue.empty(): return; temp = queue.dequeue(); reverse_queue(queue); stack.push(temp);

examples.trace: Step 1: Queue=[A,B,C], temp=A, call reverse_queue([B,C]). Step 2: Queue=[B,C], temp=B, call reverse_queue([C]). Step 3: Queue=[C], temp=C, call reverse_queue([]). Step 4: Base case, return. Step 5: Push C → Stack=[C]. Step 6: Push B → Stack=[C,B]. Step 7: Push A → Stack=[C,B,A]. Stack top=A.

Step 1: Queue=[X,Y], temp=X, call reverse_queue([Y]). Step 2: Queue=[Y], temp=Y, call reverse_queue([]). Step 3: Base case, return. Step 4: Push Y → Stack=[Y]. Step 5: Push X → Stack=[Y,X]. Stack top=X.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Defer-and-Push Pattern
ladders.desc: The technique of storing an element before recursion and pushing after
ladders.explanation: This core logic involves the specific pattern: dequeue an element, store it, make recursive call, then push it after the call returns. Intuition: By postponing the push operation until after recursion completes, we leverage the call stack's LIFO nature to reverse the order. The stored elements wait in memory while deeper recursion processes later elements first. This creates a reversal because the recursion unwinds from the innermost call (processing the last element) to the outermost call (processing the first element). Step-by-step: 1. Remove element from data structure. 2. Store it in a variable. 3. Recurse on remaining structure. 4. After recursion, perform operation with stored element. Time complexity: O(n) for n elements. Space complexity: O(n) for storage and recursion. Connection to L1: This pattern is implemented within the recursive function. Common mistakes include pushing before recursion (which doesn't reverse), losing the stored element, or not handling the base case. Real code pattern: element = data.pop(); recurse(remaining); operation(element);

examples.output: Operations in order: push 3, push 2, push 1
examples.trace: Step 1: Store 1, recurse on [2,3]. Step 2: Store 2, recurse on [3]. Step 3: Store 3, recurse on []. Step 4: Base case. Step 5: Push 3. Step 6: Push 2. Step 7: Push 1. Final order: 3,2,1 pushed.

examples.output: Operations in order: push B, push A
examples.trace: Step 1: Store A, recurse on [B]. Step 2: Store B, recurse on []. Step 3: Base case. Step 4: Push B. Step 5: Push A. Final order: B,A pushed.

testCases.expected: Push c, then b, then a
Push w, then z, then y, then x

ladders.title: Building Block — Recursion with Side Effects
ladders.desc: Using recursion that modifies external data structures
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves recursive functions that don't just return values but also cause side effects like modifying a stack or queue. Intuition: In standard recursion, we often return computed values up the call chain. Here, the recursion's purpose is to orchestrate operations in reverse order by performing actions after recursive calls return. The function itself might return void, but it alters external state (the stack). This is different from pure functional recursion. Step-by-step: 1. Define recursive function that takes a mutable data structure. 2. In function, perform operation to change structure (e.g., dequeue). 3. Recurse on modified structure. 4. After recursion, perform another operation (e.g., push). The side effects accumulate in LIFO order. Time complexity depends on operations. Space complexity includes recursion depth. Connection to L2: This enables the defer-and-push pattern. Common mistakes include not passing the modified structure correctly, infinite recursion, or side effects in wrong order. Real code pattern: def recurse_side_effect(data): if base_case: return; modify(data); recurse_side_effect(data); other_modify(data);

examples.trace: Step 1: List=[1,2,3], remove 1, recurse on [2,3]. Step 2: List=[2,3], remove 2, recurse on [3]. Step 3: List=[3], remove 3, recurse on []. Step 4: Base case. Step 5: Print 3. Step 6: Print 2. Step 7: Print 1. Output: 3 2 1.
Step 1: List=[4,5], store 4, recurse on [5]. Step 2: List=[5], store 5, recurse on []. Step 3: Base case. Step 4: Add 5 to sum (sum=5). Step 5: Add 4 to sum (sum=9). Final sum=9.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Queue Dequeue and Stack Push
ladders.desc: Fundamental operations of removing from queue front and adding to stack top
ladders.explanation: This level covers the basic operations: dequeue (remove and return the front element from a queue) and push (add an element to the top of a stack). Intuition: Queues follow FIFO: the first element enqueued is the first dequeued. Stacks follow LIFO: the last element pushed is the first popped. Dequeue reduces the queue size by one and returns the oldest element. Push increases stack size by one and places the new element on top. These operations are atomic and essential for data structure manipulation. Step-by-step for dequeue: 1. Check if queue is not empty. 2. Remove element from front. 3. Return removed element. For push: 1. Accept element as input. 2. Place element on top of stack. 3. Update stack pointer. Time complexity: O(1) for both. Space complexity: O(1). Connection to L3: These operations are used within recursive functions. Common mistakes include dequeue from empty queue (underflow), not handling return values, or confusing order. Real code pattern: queue.dequeue() returns front element; stack.push(element) adds to top.

examples.trace: Initial queue front=10. Dequeue operation removes 10 and returns it. Queue now has front=20, elements [20,30].
Initial stack top=5. Push 15 adds 15 on top. Stack now has elements [5,15], top=15.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — FIFO vs LIFO Order
ladders.explanation: This foundational concept explains the core difference between queue and stack ordering. Intuition: A queue is like a line of people: the first person to join is the first to leave (FIFO). A stack is like a stack of plates: the last plate added is the first to be removed (LIFO). In computing, queues are used for tasks like printing jobs, while stacks are used for function calls or undo operations. Key insight: The order of elements is determined by how they are added and removed. For a queue, enqueue adds to back, dequeue removes from front. For a stack, push adds to top, pop removes from top. This difference is crucial for the conversion problem because we need to change from one order to the other. Step-by-step: 1. Identify input sequence. 2. Apply queue rules: elements leave in same order they entered. 3. Apply stack rules: elements leave in reverse order they entered. Time/space: Conceptual, no complexity. Connection to L4: This understanding guides which operations to use. Common mistakes include confusing which end is which, or misapplying the rules. Real code pattern: Queue: add to back, remove from front. Stack: add to top, remove from top.

examples.input: Queue with elements added in order: A, B, C
examples.trace: Enqueue A → Queue=[A]. Enqueue B → Queue=[A,B]. Enqueue C → Queue=[A,B,C]. Dequeue → returns A, Queue=[B,C]. Dequeue → returns B, Queue=[C]. Dequeue → returns C, Queue=[].

examples.input: Stack with elements pushed in order: X, Y, Z
examples.trace: Push X → Stack=[X]. Push Y → Stack=[X,Y]. Push Z → Stack=[X,Y,Z]. Pop → returns Z, Stack=[X,Y]. Pop → returns Y, Stack=[X]. Pop → returns X, Stack=[].

testCases.input: Queue: add a, add b, add c, dequeue twice
Stack: push x, push y, push z, pop twice
Queue: add 3, add 4, add 5, dequeue all
Stack: push a, push b, pop all
Queue: add 0, add 1, dequeue, add 2, dequeue
